eras.name: Habsburg Imperial Splendor
eras.description: The Habsburg court in Vienna set the standard for Central European dress with Spanish-influenced formality and lavish use of silk, fur, and precious metals. Court protocol dictated fashion with extraordinary rigidity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 16th-century Habsburg emperor in a black velvet doublet slashed to reveal gold silk underneath, wearing a massive white lace ruff and a jeweled Order of the Golden Fleece pendant, in a Vienna palace throne room.

eras.name: Rococo Vienna
eras.description: Under Empress Maria Theresa, Vienna rivaled Versailles in opulence. Austrian Rococo fashion featured lighter pastel fabrics, elaborate wigs, and jeweled accessories while the empress herself used dress as a tool of statecraft.
eras.aiImagePrompt: An 18th-century Viennese court scene: a noblewoman in a wide-skirted pastel pink silk gown with silver embroidery and lace trim, powdered hair adorned with pearls, in a chandelier-lit Schönbrunn Palace ballroom.

eras.name: Biedermeier Domesticity
eras.description: Post-Napoleonic austerity gave rise to the Biedermeier period, emphasizing modest, practical bourgeois dress. Women favored full sleeves and natural waists, while men adopted the frock coat as everyday wear.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Biedermeier-era Viennese couple in a parlor: the woman in a high-waisted muslin dress with enormous puffed sleeves and a lace cap, the man in a dark frock coat, soft natural window light.

eras.name: Fin-de-Siècle Vienna
eras.description: Vienna became Europe's cultural cauldron. The Wiener Werkstätte revolutionized fashion by merging fine art with clothing design, while Gustav Klimt and Emilie Flöge created avant-garde reform dresses rejecting corsetry.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Secessionist-era woman in 1905 Vienna wearing a flowing, uncorseted Klimt-inspired reform dress with geometric gold and turquoise patterns, standing in a Wiener Werkstätte interior with Josef Hoffmann furniture.

eras.name: Tracht Revival and Alpine Identity
eras.description: Alpine folk dress was codified and romanticized as a symbol of Austrian identity. The Dirndl and Lederhosen transitioned from rural workwear to national costume, surviving both the interwar period and post-war reconstruction.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1930s Austrian alpine scene: a woman in a traditional Dirndl with checked apron and puffed-sleeve blouse, a man in Lederhosen with embroidered suspenders, standing in a flower-filled Tyrolean meadow with mountain peaks.

eras.name: Viennese Design Modernism
eras.description: A new wave of Austrian designers blends Helmut Lang's radical minimalism with sustainable Alpine materials. Vienna's fashion scene emphasizes intellectual, concept-driven design that bridges heritage craft and contemporary architecture.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary Viennese fashion editorial in a Brutalist museum courtyard: a model in an architecturally cut charcoal Loden coat with asymmetric closure, paired with sleek black trousers, overcast light.

textiles.name: Loden
textiles.description: A dense, waterproof boiled wool fabric originating in the Tyrolean Alps, renowned for its durability and weather resistance. It remains a cornerstone of Austrian outerwear.
textiles.technique: Wool is woven then repeatedly fulled (boiled and beaten) to create a dense, water-repellent felt

textiles.name: Viennese Silk Brocade
textiles.description: Luxurious silk brocade with metallic thread supplementary weft patterns, historically produced for Habsburg court gowns and ecclesiastical vestments.
textiles.technique: Jacquard weaving with silk warp and metallic gold or silver supplementary weft

textiles.name: Tyrolean Linen
textiles.description: Crisp, handwoven linen used for Dirndl blouses and traditional shirts, often bleached white and finished with delicate drawn-thread embroidery.
textiles.technique: Flax fiber spun and woven on narrow looms, then bleached on alpine meadows

textiles.name: Gmundner Embroidery
textiles.description: Intricate whitework embroidery on fine linen, featuring floral and geometric motifs specific to the Salzkammergut lake region.
textiles.technique: Hand-stitched cutwork and drawn-thread embroidery on fine white linen

textiles.name: Wiener Werkstätte Printed Silk
textiles.description: Bold, geometric and floral printed silks designed by artists of the Wiener Werkstätte, bridging fine art and fashion in the early 20th century.
textiles.technique: Hand-printed silk using woodblock and later screen printing with artist-designed patterns

climate.fashionImplication: Cold alpine winters and significant snowfall demand heavy wool outerwear, Loden cloaks, and substantial leather boots. The continental climate produces hot summers in eastern lowlands, requiring lighter seasonal wardrobes.

culturalInfluences: Habsburg multi-ethnic empire and court protocol
Wiener Werkstätte and Secessionist art movement
Alpine pastoral traditions and Tracht customs
Classical music and opera costume traditions
Post-war reconstruction and Helmut Lang's minimalism
